title: Aristotle, Kant, and Evolution
essence: Aristotle accepts Plato's diagnosis — the psyche is divided, inner conflict is real — but rejects the two-worlds solution. Instead of anagoge toward a separate realm of Forms, Aristotle proposes a this-worldly developmental account: virtue is acquired through practice, not contemplation of transcendent truth; the soul's actualization occurs through engagement with the world, not escape from it. Vervaeke uses Aristotle's concepts of form/matter and actuality/potentiality to show that Aristotle's metaphysics is fundamentally about development — things have an inherent trajectory toward their proper actualization. The challenge is explaining why development fails: why do people fail to actualize their potential? Here Vervaeke introduces Alicia Juarrero's dynamical systems account of complex adaptive systems and the concept of the 'virtual engine' — a system of enabling and selective constraints that creates a feedback loop between organism and environment, making growth and self-organization possible. Akrasia (weakness of will, acting against one's better judgment) is analyzed as a failure of the virtual engine — a breakdown in the constraint system that normally coordinates appetite and reason. Character, for Aristotle, is the stabilized pattern of virtual engine functioning that makes reliable virtue possible. The episode bridges ancient virtue ethics to contemporary complexity theory and cognitive science.
keyClaims: Aristotle accepts Plato's diagnosis of psychic division but rejects the two-worlds solution in favor of this-worldly developmental actualization.
Form and matter in Aristotle's metaphysics describe a developmental trajectory: things have an inherent direction toward their proper actualization.
Juarrero's virtual engine — a system of enabling and selective constraints in a feedback loop — provides a dynamical systems account of how growth and self-organization are possible.
Akrasia is a failure of the virtual engine: a breakdown in the constraint system that normally coordinates appetite and reason toward good action.
Character is the stabilized pattern of virtual engine functioning that makes reliable virtue possible — it is a kind of second nature.

concepts.summary: Aristotelian virtue as acquired through practice rather than contemplation of Forms — a second nature achieved through habituated action that stabilizes the virtual engine.
Weakness of will — acting against one's better judgment — analyzed as a failure of the virtual engine, a breakdown in the constraint system coordinating appetite and reason.
Phronesis (practical wisdom) as Aristotle's alternative to Platonic anagoge — the capacity to discern the right action in context, developed through habituation and experience.
Aristotle accepts Plato's diagnosis that the psyche is genuinely divided — the task is integrating the parts through virtuous practice, not escaping to a higher realm.
The form/matter distinction reread as describing the structural-functional organization of things — what something is organized to do, its proper trajectory of actualization.
Juarrero's dynamical systems account connects Aristotle's development to contemporary 4E cognition — cognition as embodied, embedded, enacted, and extended.
Aristotle's developmental account represents an alternative meaning-making framework to Plato's — but one that will also be disrupted by historical transformations.

people.summary: Aristotle's metaphysics (form/matter, actuality/potentiality), his rejection of Platonic two-worlds, his developmental ethics, and his analysis of akrasia all examined in detail.
Alicia Juarrero's dynamical systems account of complex adaptive systems and the 'virtual engine' concept introduced as a contemporary analog to Aristotle's developmental metaphysics.
Kant's categorical imperative discussed as an attempt to ground morality in pure practical reason — contrasted with Aristotle's virtue ethics as competing responses to the same problem.
Francisco Varela's work on autopoiesis and enactivism referenced in relation to Juarrero's dynamical systems account.
Evan Thompson's enactivist cognitive science referenced alongside Varela as contemporary support for Aristotle's embedded developmental account.
Plato's two-worlds solution examined as the foil against which Aristotle develops his this-worldly alternative.

crossRefs.context: Aristotle is the student introduced at the end of episode 5 as the critic of Plato's two-worlds solution; this episode develops that criticism into an alternative.
Aristotle's developmental account is further extended in episode 7 through the agent-arena relationship and Fromm's having/being modes.
Akrasia is the philosophical analysis of what Socratic practice aimed to cure — the failure to act in accordance with one's better judgment.
